_purpose: Courthouse Atlas for Cass County, TX. Record survival and courthouse history. Cass County records date from 1846 organization. No major fire losses. Marion County was carved from Cass in 1860 — early Jefferson-area records are here.

previous_courthouses.fate: No significant fire damage. Replaced by current WPA-era building.

record_losses.description: Cass County's courthouse has not experienced a major fire. Records are continuous from county organization in 1846. Important note: Cass County originally included what is now Marion County. Records for the Jefferson area from 1846 to 1860 (when Marion County was created) are in Cass County's files.
record_losses.narrative_impact: Cass County's records are intact from 1846. If {ancestor_name} was in the Jefferson area before 1860, the records are here at the Linden courthouse, not in Marion County.
record_losses.source: Texas State Library and Archives Commission. County Records Inventory.

deeds.completeness: Good from county organization. Includes pre-1860 deeds for the Jefferson/Marion County area.

vital_records.repository: Texas Department of State Health Services

substitute_sources.description: Population schedules 1850–1940. The 1850 census includes the Jefferson area (before Marion County was created). Slave schedules (1850, 1860) list slaveholders.
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Essential for tracking families. The 1850 Cass County census includes the Jefferson area, making it critical for families who later appear in Marion County records.

substitute_sources.description: Records from the Hughes Iron Works and other Cass County iron operations are fragmentary but some survive. They document the antebellum industrial workforce.
substitute_sources.repository: East Texas Research Center, SFA; private collections
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Documents ironworkers — both enslaved and free — in one of the few industrial operations in antebellum East Texas.
substitute_sources.source: Dunn, 'Iron Making in Nineteenth-Century East Texas.'

narrative_hooks: Cass County's records include the Jefferson area from 1846 to 1860 — if {ancestor_name} was in Jefferson before Marion County was created, the deeds and court records are in Linden, not Jefferson.
The Cass County courthouse has no major fire losses — {ancestor_name}'s records from 1846 onward are almost certainly preserved.
Texas vital records start in 1903 and were unreliable in rural areas until ~1910 — the absence of a birth record for {ancestor_name} in Cass County before that date is standard.
If {ancestor_name} worked at the Hughes Iron Works, court records and census schedules are the primary documentation — the furnace's own records are fragmentary.
source_references: Texas State Library and Archives Commission. County Records Inventory.
Dunn, Jeff. 'Iron Making in Nineteenth-Century East Texas.' East Texas Historical Journal 33, no. 1 (1995).
Works Progress Administration. Historical Records Survey: Texas County Records. Austin, 1937–1942.
